source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Consider the Young's interference experiment shown in the diagram (Fig. 2.7). Assume that the wavelength of the light is $6000 \text{Å}$, the slit widths are all the same, $S_{0}=S_{1}=S_{2}=0.2 \text{mm}$, the slit separation $d=2.0 \text{mm}$, and $L_{1}=3.0 \text{m}$. About how long must $L_{0}$ be in order to produce a well-defined interference pattern on the screen?

candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: Consider the Young's interference experiment shown in the diagram (Fig. 2.7). Assume that the wavelength of the light is $6000 \text{Å}$, the slit widths are all the same, $S_{0}=S_{1}=S_{2}=0.2 \text{mm}$, the slit separation $d=2.0 \text{mm}$, and $L_{1}=3.0 \text{m}$. candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object representation rather than a real file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain:
According to the Van Cittert-Zernike theorem, the lateral coherence length of an extended slit source is given by
$$
l_{s} = \frac{\lambda}{\theta_{s}},
$$
where $\theta_{s}$ is the angle subtended by the slit at the point concerned in radians. 

In order to get a clear interference pattern, the distance between $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ should be less than $l_{s}$, i.e.,
$$
d < l_{s},
$$
or
$$
\theta_{s} < \frac{\lambda}{d}.
$$

As
$$
\theta_{s} = \frac{S_{0}}{L_{0}}
$$
we require that
$$
L_{0} > \frac{d}{\lambda} S_{0} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{6000 \times 10^{-10}} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.67\,\text{m}.
$$
